tech-ai.title: Musk mulled handing OpenAI to his children, Altman testifies
tech-ai.summary: Elon Musk, the billionaire founder of Tesla and SpaceX, is currently in a lawsuit against OpenAI (the company behind ChatGPT) and its CEO Sam Altman. During the trial, Altman testified that Musk once considered giving OpenAI to his own children to control, which alarmed Altman. Altman explained that OpenAI was specifically created to prevent any single person from controlling advanced AI technology—a safeguard meant to protect humanity. This matters because it reveals a fundamental disagreement about who should control powerful AI systems: should they be owned by one person (like Musk might want) or spread across many people for safety? The lawsuit highlights a major split between two of tech's biggest figures over AI's future.

tech-ai.title: Sam Altman says Elon Musk's mind games were damaging OpenAI
tech-ai.summary: During his testimony in the lawsuit against him, Sam Altman revealed that Elon Musk's management style caused serious damage to OpenAI's workplace culture. According to Altman, Musk demanded that OpenAI's leadership rank researchers by accomplishments and "take a chainsaw through" (meaning cut) many of them—basically firing people to reduce costs. While this aggressive cost-cutting approach works at Tesla, Altman argued it doesn't fit an AI research company where teamwork and trust are essential. This lawsuit is important because it shows how the two most powerful figures in AI technology fundamentally disagree about values, leadership, and how AI companies should operate. Their conflict will likely shape how AI companies are run in the future.

tech-ai.title: Medicare's new payment model is built for AI, and most of the tech world has no idea
tech-ai.summary: Medicare, the U.S. government's health insurance program for older people, just created a new payment system specifically designed for AI healthcare tools. Until now, there was no official way for hospitals to bill Medicare when an AI agent (a software program that acts independently) monitors patients between doctor visits, reminds them to take medication, or arranges housing help. This new system called ACCESS changes everything by creating official payment pathways for AI healthcare. Why does this matter to you? It means AI will soon play a much bigger role in your parents' or grandparents' healthcare, potentially catching health problems earlier and improving care quality. However, it also raises questions about whether AI healthcare workers will be as good as human doctors.

tech-ai.title: Report: Google and SpaceX in talks to put data centers into orbit
tech-ai.summary: Google and SpaceX—two of the world's largest tech companies—are reportedly discussing building data centers (massive computer facilities that store and process data) in space. A data center is essentially thousands of computers working together; currently, they're all on Earth and require tons of electricity and cooling water. The idea is to put them in orbit using SpaceX's rockets, where space's cold temperatures could reduce cooling costs. However, this is still very expensive compared to Earth-based data centers. This matters because as AI becomes more powerful, it needs increasingly massive amounts of computing power, and companies like Google are looking for creative (if futuristic) solutions. It also shows how intertwined space exploration and AI technology are becoming.

tech-ai.title: Everything Google announced at its Android Show, from Googlebooks to vibe-coded widgets
tech-ai.summary: Google announced multiple major products and features at its Android Show event. Most importantly, Google revealed "Googlebooks," a new line of laptops coming this fall that will replace Chromebooks—Google's previous laptop line. Googlebooks will run a new operating system combining Android and Chrome OS into one system. Google also showed off AI-powered features like smarter Gemini assistance (Gemini is Google's AI chatbot), customizable widgets with AI-generated designs, improved voice dictation, and other AI-first tools across its devices. This is significant because it shows Google's strategy of embedding AI into everything you touch—your phone, laptop, and apps. For teenagers, this means your devices will soon be much more AI-powered, helping you write essays, edit photos, and navigate your phone hands-free.

tech-ai.title: Meta will tell parents when their teens add new interests to their Instagram algorithm
tech-ai.summary: Meta (the company behind Instagram and Facebook) is rolling out a new parental control feature that lets parents see what their teenage kids are interested in on Instagram. Parents can now view general topics their teens engage with—like "basketball" or "fashion"—and Meta will notify parents whenever a teen adds a new interest to their algorithm. An algorithm is basically a set of rules that decides what content you see; Instagram's algorithm learns from your interests to show you more similar content. This feature matters to you directly because it changes your privacy on Instagram: your parents might now see information about your interests that you didn't explicitly share with them. While parents say this helps them keep kids safe, many teenagers see it as invading privacy, creating tension between safety and personal freedom.

tech-ai.title: Meta won't let you block its AI account on Threads
tech-ai.summary: Meta announced a new feature on Threads (its Twitter-like social platform) that lets users tag a Meta AI account to ask questions or get context about conversations. However, users discovered they cannot block this AI account—a feature that normally lets you prevent someone from contacting you or seeing your posts. Meta is testing this similar to how Elon Musk's X platform has Grok (an AI that answers questions). The inability to block Meta AI frustrates users who don't want forced AI interactions on the platform. This matters because it shows a troubling tech trend: major companies are pushing AI into your social media experience whether you want it or not, and they're removing traditional safety tools (like blocking) that give users control. It raises important questions about whether tech companies should be allowed to force AI interactions on unwilling users.

tech-ai.title: Potholes cost cities millions: This company is using AI and trucks to fix them
tech-ai.summary: Samsara, a fleet management company (a company that helps businesses track and manage their vehicles), has developed an AI system that detects potholes and predicts how fast they're getting worse. Potholes—those annoying broken sections of road—cost U.S. cities millions of dollars annually in repairs and vehicle damage. Samsara's AI can analyze data from trucks driving around cities to identify problem areas before they become dangerous, allowing cities to fix them proactively rather than reactively. This is a practical example of how AI solves real-world problems you deal with every day. Instead of just waiting for a pothole to damage your parents' car, AI can predict and prevent it. It also shows how AI isn't just fancy chatbots; it's being used to make infrastructure safer and reduce taxpayer costs.

tech-ai.title: The AI legal services industry is heating up — Anthropic is getting in on the action
tech-ai.summary: Anthropic, an AI safety company that created Claude (a popular AI chatbot competitor to ChatGPT), is launching new AI tools specifically designed for law firms. These tools automate boring clerical work that lawyers normally do manually, including searching through legal documents, reviewing contracts, researching case law, preparing depositions (witness interviews), and drafting legal documents. A deposition is a formal question-and-answer session where lawyers gather evidence. This matters because it shows how AI is moving beyond consumer products (like ChatGPT) into professional industries. Lawyers spend enormous time on paperwork, and AI could free them up for actual legal strategy and client interaction. However, it also raises concerns about whether AI will replace junior lawyers' jobs—the entry-level positions where many people start legal careers.

tech-ai.title: Kevin Hartz's A* just closed its third fund with $450M
tech-ai.summary: Kevin Hartz, a successful tech entrepreneur, announced that his venture capital firm A* (pronounced "A-Star") has raised $450 million for its third fund. Venture capital firms are basically investment companies that give money to new startups in exchange for ownership; a "fund" is a pool of money they collect from investors to deploy. A* takes a generalist approach, meaning it invests across many tech areas including AI applications, fintech (financial technology), healthcare, and cybersecurity. With this fund, A* plans to back at least 30 startups with average investments between $3-5 million each. This matters because it shows investors are still confident in AI and tech startups, even during uncertain economic times. The more venture capital flowing into AI, the faster new AI products and services will be created, affecting what products you'll use in the coming years.

science-space.title: SpaceX's Starship V3 megarocket finally has a debut launch date
science-space.summary: SpaceX, the private space company founded by billionaire Elon Musk, has announced that its most advanced rocket yet—Starship V3—will launch on May 19, 2026. This megarocket is a massive stepping stone toward humanity's biggest dreams: landing astronauts on the moon and eventually sending humans to Mars. Think of Starship like a giant, reusable spaceship that can carry both cargo and people to places we've never been before. Unlike traditional rockets that fall back to Earth and explode, Starship is designed to land itself safely so it can be used again and again, which could make space travel much cheaper. This launch represents a major milestone in making space exploration more accessible and getting humanity closer to becoming a multi-planet species.

science-space.title: NASA's Next-Gen Space Processor Undergoes Testing
science-space.summary: NASA's High Performance Spaceflight Computing project is developing powerful new computer chips designed specifically for spacecraft—think of these as the brains of space missions. Currently, spacecraft use older, slower computer chips because they've been proven to survive the extreme conditions of space: intense radiation, extreme temperatures, and the vacuum that can destroy regular electronics. However, these older chips limit what missions can accomplish—they can't process information fast enough for truly autonomous spacecraft (robots that can make decisions on their own without waiting for instructions from Earth). The new processors being tested will dramatically boost computing power while still withstanding the harsh space environment, enabling future rovers and spacecraft to explore faster, make smarter decisions independently, and help scientists discover new things about planets and space at unprecedented speeds.

science-space.title: 'Cannibal stars,' AI and the Rubin Observatory could solve the dark energy mystery
science-space.summary: Scientists are using artificial intelligence (AI) and the Rubin Observatory—a powerful telescope under construction—to solve one of the biggest mysteries in the universe: dark energy, which makes up about 68% of everything that exists. They're studying 'Type 1a supernovas' (explosions of dying stars called 'cannibal stars' because they steal material from nearby stars), which scientists traditionally used as reliable measuring sticks to map the universe's expansion. However, researchers now suspect these supernovas aren't as standard as previously thought, meaning we might be missing important information about dark energy. By combining AI's ability to spot hidden patterns with massive telescope data, scientists hope to find these 'unknown unknowns'—the missing clues that could finally explain why the universe is expanding faster and faster, fundamentally changing how we understand reality itself.

science-space.title: Uranus and Neptune could be full of rocks, not just ice
science-space.summary: A surprising new study challenges what we've believed about Uranus and Neptune for decades. Scientists have long called these distant planets 'ice giants' because we thought they were made primarily of frozen water and other ices surrounding a rocky core. However, new research suggests these planets might actually contain far more solid rock throughout their interiors than previously assumed, making them fundamentally different from what textbooks described. This discovery matters because understanding what these planets are made of helps us understand how they formed billions of years ago and how similar planets around distant stars might be structured. The findings suggest scientists should rethink planetary classifications entirely—rather than just calling planets 'icy' or 'rocky,' we need a more nuanced system that reflects the complex compositions astronomers are discovering.

science-space.title: Star Catcher raises $65 million to build space's first laser power grid
science-space.summary: Star Catcher Industries just secured $65 million in funding to build something revolutionary: the world's first space-based power grid using lasers. Here's how it would work: satellites orbiting Earth would beam power down to the planet using laser technology, kind of like wireless charging but for entire cities or space stations. This might sound like science fiction, but the company believes it could transform the economy by providing clean, constant energy to remote areas and supporting space-based industries like manufacturing and mining on the moon. The challenge is developing satellites that can safely and efficiently transmit massive amounts of power over vast distances without accidentally zapping things below. If successful, this technology could revolutionize how we power Earth and make space-based industries economically viable by providing the abundant energy they need.

science-space.title: Cowboy Space raises $275 million for AI data centers in orbit
science-space.summary: Cowboy Space Corp., a newly rebranded company, just raised an enormous $275 million to accomplish something ambitious: launch artificial intelligence (AI) data centers into space on a rocket they're developing themselves. Data centers are massive facilities packed with computers that process and store information—think of them as the brains that run everything from Netflix to your email. By placing these in orbit, Cowboy Space could offer AI computing services from space, which has advantages like reduced cooling costs (space is incredibly cold) and protection from earthly disasters. The company plans to design and build their own custom rocket specifically for these launches, which is ambitious but could lead to innovative transportation solutions. If successful, this could create an entirely new industry: space-based computing infrastructure that serves companies on Earth while potentially supporting future space exploration and colonization efforts.

science-space.title: Wildfire pollution causes thousands of deaths yearly, satellite data reveals
science-space.summary: New satellite data analysis reveals a hidden health crisis: wildfire-derived ozone is responsible for approximately 2,045 excess deaths per year across the United States. When wildfires burn, they release chemicals that react in the atmosphere to create ground-level ozone—a pollutant invisible to the naked eye but extremely harmful when breathed into lungs. This differs from the ozone layer high in the atmosphere that protects us from the sun; this pollution stays low and affects people directly. Scientists analyzed 20 years of satellite data tracking ozone patterns and correlated them with death records to make this discovery, which had previously been hidden because the connection wasn't obvious. This finding is critical because it means climate change and increasing wildfires pose a direct threat to public health that governments and health agencies need to address urgently through better air quality protections and wildfire prevention strategies.

science-space.title: NASA's Psyche asteroid probe will fly within 3,000 miles of Mars
science-space.summary: NASA's Psyche spacecraft is heading for a close encounter with Mars on May 15, 2026, passing within just 3,000 miles of the planet's surface. The Psyche mission is on its way to study an asteroid called Psyche in the asteroid belt (the region between Mars and Jupiter filled with rocky objects), which scientists believe might be the metallic core of a failed planet—essentially a chunk of iron and nickel left over from the early solar system. This Mars flyby isn't the destination; it's a gravity assist, which is like a cosmic slingshot that uses Mars's gravity to boost the spacecraft's speed and redirect it toward the asteroid without using extra fuel. By studying Psyche, scientists hope to learn how planets form and what Earth's hidden metal core might be like. This mission represents cutting-edge planetary exploration and demonstrates NASA's strategy of using planetary flybys to accomplish ambitious deep-space missions efficiently.

science-space.title: NASA's Perseverance Rover snaps stunning new selfie on Mars
science-space.summary: NASA's Perseverance rover, which has been exploring Mars since 2021, recently took an impressive self-portrait at a location scientists nicknamed 'Lac de Charmes' during its westward journey beyond Jezero Crater. The selfie was assembled from 61 individual images stitched together by engineers—similar to how you might combine multiple photos into a panoramic shot with your phone. In the photo, Perseverance shows off its mast camera pointed at a rocky outcrop where the rover had just created a circular abrasion patch by grinding away the surface to examine fresh rock underneath. This type of close-up investigation helps scientists understand Mars's geological history and search for signs of ancient microbial life. These selfies serve a practical purpose too: they help engineers monitor the rover's condition and check for damage from the harsh Martian environment, ensuring this six-wheeled explorer continues its groundbreaking mission.

science-space.title: I Am Artemis: Meet Kathleen Harmon, NASA's Artemis program leader
science-space.summary: Kathleen Harmon is the Artemis II Mission Interface Manager for NASA's Deep Space Network, a crucial role in humanity's return to the moon. Harmon's journey is inspiring: as a child, she was captivated by Apollo moon landing launches on television, dreaming of space exploration, and now she's helping make the next chapter of lunar missions a reality. The Artemis program aims to land the first woman and first person of color on the moon, marking a massive step forward for space exploration and representation in STEM fields. The Deep Space Network is a global system of giant radio antennas that communicate with spacecraft millions of miles away, and Harmon's job is ensuring flawless communication during this critical mission. Her story illustrates how childhood wonder about space can transform into a career making real discoveries, and it shows that NASA's future depends on talented people like her who bridge the gap between groundbreaking engineering and achieving humanity's greatest ambitions.

finance-markets.title: US inflation jumps to 3.8% as Trump's Iran war sends petrol prices soaring
finance-markets.summary: The US inflation rate—the speed at which prices rise for everyday goods—jumped to 3.8% in April, the highest level in three years. This matters because when inflation is high, your money doesn't buy as much stuff. The main culprit? An ongoing conflict between the US and Iran that's driving up oil prices worldwide. Since America is the world's biggest economy, when US prices surge, it affects global markets and people's wallets everywhere. The Federal Reserve (the US central bank that controls interest rates) now faces pressure to decide whether to raise rates, which could slow the economy but fight inflation. This affects teenagers because higher prices mean less purchasing power—think more expensive gas, food, and everyday items.

finance-markets.title: Kevin Warsh to face resurgent inflation and an impatient Trump as Fed chair
finance-markets.summary: Kevin Warsh, a 56-year-old financier and banker, is about to become the new head of the Federal Reserve (the powerful US central bank that controls interest rates and money policy). He's replacing Jay Powell, the outgoing chair. This is huge because the Fed controls how expensive it is for people and businesses to borrow money, which ripples through the entire economy. Warsh faces two big challenges: fighting the rising inflation we just discussed, plus dealing with President Trump, who has publicly pressured Fed leaders to cut interest rates to boost the economy. The tension here is real—Warsh needs to make independent decisions about inflation, but Trump wants lower rates. This job basically shapes whether your future student loans will be expensive or affordable.

finance-markets.title: UK borrowing costs surge as Starmer leadership crisis rattles bond markets
finance-markets.summary: The UK is facing a political crisis as Prime Minister Keir Starmer (who leads the British government) loses control of his cabinet while inflation and economic problems mount. When political instability happens, investors get nervous and demand higher interest rates to lend money to the government—this is called borrowing costs rising. The 30-year gilt yield (a "gilt" is a UK government bond, basically a loan to the government) just hit its highest level this century, meaning Britain is paying more to borrow. This matters globally because the UK is a major financial hub; when British stability shakes, it affects worldwide markets. For teenagers, unstable governments struggle to invest in schools, healthcare, and infrastructure. Political chaos + economic weakness = lower confidence in the country's future, which spreads fear through financial markets.

finance-markets.title: Trump's plan to discuss Taiwan arms sales with Xi rattles Asian allies
finance-markets.summary: President Trump is planning to negotiate with Chinese President Xi Jinping about reducing US weapons sales to Taiwan (a self-governed island that China claims as its own). This is unprecedented and makes America's Asian allies—Japan, South Korea, Australia—extremely nervous. The US has sold defensive weapons to Taiwan for decades as a way to balance China's military power in the region. If Trump pulls back, it could embolden China to take aggressive action. Why does this matter financially? Geopolitical instability (conflict between nations) tanks stock markets and disrupts global trade. Taiwan produces over 60% of the world's semiconductors (computer chips needed for everything from phones to cars). Any conflict there would cripple the global supply chain and crater economies worldwide. This negotiation could reshape Asian security and reshape trillions of dollars in global commerce.

finance-markets.title: Blue Owl retail fundraising evaporates amid private credit concerns
finance-markets.summary: Blue Owl, a major investment firm, is struggling to raise money for its funds because investors are getting scared about "private credit"—basically loans that large investment firms make to businesses instead of traditional banks. The firm's fundraising dropped dramatically compared to last year because people worry about a potential wave of loan defaults (when borrowers can't pay back their loans). This signals a bigger problem: the private credit market is huge (hundreds of billions of dollars) and largely unregulated, meaning there's less oversight than traditional banking. When loan defaults spike, it can cause a financial crisis. Why should a teenager care? If private credit implodes, it could trigger an economic recession, meaning job losses, lower college funding, and reduced opportunities. This story shows cracks forming in an important but shadowy corner of global finance that most people don't know about.

finance-markets.title: China's big tech groups miss out on AI stock market frenzy
finance-markets.summary: Major Chinese tech companies like Tencent and Alibaba are being left behind as global investors rush into "pure AI plays" (companies focused entirely on artificial intelligence). While the rest of the world is investing heavily in AI stocks, China's tech giants—despite being hugely powerful in their home market—aren't capturing this investment wave. This happens partly because US-China tensions make Western investors nervous about Chinese tech, and partly because Beijing regulates AI heavily. This matters because it shows how geopolitical conflict (US-China tension) directly impacts which companies succeed financially. The AI boom is reshaping global wealth; if Chinese companies get locked out, China's economy could lose trillions in future growth. For teenagers, it means the tech jobs and innovation of the future might concentrate in Western countries, shifting global economic power.

finance-markets.title: India's Economic Strength Defies Foreign Outflow Fears, S&P Says
finance-markets.summary: India's economy is performing better than worried investors think, according to S&P Global Ratings (a major credit-rating agency that evaluates how risky investments are). Many people feared that foreign investors would pull money out of India, but that's not happening as badly as expected. India is growing faster than most developed countries and has strong fundamentals despite global financial turbulence. This is important because India is home to nearly 2 billion people and is becoming a major global economic player. If India stays stable and grows, it creates opportunities for global investment and trade. For teenagers, a strong India means more tech jobs (India's IT industry is world-class), more international business opportunities, and a counterweight to China's economic dominance in Asia. India's strength is reshaping where global wealth and power flow.

finance-markets.title: Musk made 'hair-raising' demands for control of OpenAI, Altman testifies
finance-markets.summary: Elon Musk (the billionaire CEO of Tesla and SpaceX) is in a legal battle with Sam Altman (CEO of OpenAI, the AI company behind ChatGPT). Altman testified in court that Musk made extreme demands to control OpenAI, which Altman found shocking. This lawsuit reveals the messy reality behind the AI boom—these aren't calm, rational decisions but battles between billionaires fighting for control of trillion-dollar technology. Musk originally helped found OpenAI but left when it shifted toward commercial goals. Now he's suing to gain control. This matters financially because OpenAI's technology is reshaping industries, and whoever controls it holds enormous power and potential wealth. For teenagers interested in tech, this case shows how even revolutionary companies are battlegrounds for ego and control. It also raises questions about whether billionaires should have this much power over transformative technology.

finance-markets.title: Indonesia Vows 'Smart Interventions' as Rupiah Falls
finance-markets.summary: Indonesia's currency (the rupiah) has fallen to a record low, meaning it's worth less compared to other currencies like the US dollar. The Indonesian central bank (equivalent to the Federal Reserve in the US) is stepping in with "smart interventions"—carefully targeted actions to stabilize the currency. When a currency collapses, it makes imports more expensive (hurting consumers) and can trigger inflation and economic instability. Indonesia is a major economy in Southeast Asia with 270 million people, so currency weakness there ripples across the region. The rupiah's collapse likely stems from global financial uncertainty, rising US interest rates making US investments more attractive, and investors moving money out of emerging markets. For teenagers, this shows how interconnected global finance is—a currency crisis in Indonesia can eventually affect prices of imported goods worldwide, including in your country.

finance-markets.title: Stocks Pare Losses, Bonds Fall After Inflation: Markets Wrap
finance-markets.summary: Asian stock markets initially dropped after the US reported higher-than-expected inflation, but "dip buyers" (investors who buy when prices fall, betting they'll recover) stepped in to buy cheap stocks. Meanwhile, bonds fell as investors worried the Federal Reserve might raise interest rates to fight inflation. This creates a tricky situation for markets: stocks want low rates to make borrowing cheap, but fighting inflation requires higher rates. When the US releases inflation data, it shakes markets globally because the US economy is the world's largest and most influential. This particular day shows how sensitive markets are to inflation news—even temporarily, stocks panic before recovering. For teenagers, this demonstrates that stock markets swing wildly based on economic data releases. It also shows why inflation matters: it forces central banks to make hard choices between protecting savers and keeping borrowing affordable for businesses and future homebuyers.

world-news.title: Trump set to meet with Xi in Beijing as war and inflation weigh on his presidency
world-news.summary: President Donald Trump is traveling to Beijing to meet with Chinese leader Xi Jinping, marking a critical moment in US-China relations. The two countries are the world's superpowers—meaning they have the most military and economic influence globally. This visit happens during tense times: the Iran war is causing energy prices to spike (making gas and heating more expensive), inflation is rising, and both nations are competing over artificial intelligence technology. Trade tensions between the US and China affect everything from phone prices to clothing costs for teenagers worldwide. Trump and Xi will likely discuss tariffs (taxes on imported goods), military conflicts in the Middle East, and AI development. This meeting matters because decisions made here influence global stability and what products cost at stores everywhere.

world-news.title: Iran war live: Trump travels to China as conflict with Tehran looms large
world-news.summary: A major conflict is escalating between Israel and Iran, with serious consequences spreading across the Middle East. Israeli attacks on Lebanon—a neighboring country—have killed nearly 2,900 people and injured over 8,700 since March 2026. Lebanon is caught in the middle of this conflict because it borders Israel and hosts groups supported by Iran. This war is affecting the entire world: oil prices are rising (making gas more expensive), inflation is increasing, and global supply chains are disrupted (meaning products take longer to arrive in stores). The conflict also impacts politics worldwide—President Trump is heading to China partly because this war is a major international concern. Understanding this matters because Middle Eastern conflicts affect gas prices, job markets, and global stability that impact teenagers' futures everywhere.

world-news.title: US inflation jumps to 3.8% as energy costs surge from Iran war
world-news.summary: Inflation in the United States has jumped to 3.8%, the highest level in about three years, and the Iran war is a major reason why. Inflation means the same dollar buys less stuff—if inflation is high, video games, clothes, and food all cost more money. Energy costs (oil, gas, electricity) are surging because the Middle East conflict is disrupting oil supplies worldwide. When energy gets expensive, everything becomes more expensive because companies pay more to transport goods and power factories. This directly affects teenagers: your family's grocery bills are higher, gas costs more, and college savings buy less. The US Federal Reserve (the bank that controls money policy) will likely respond by raising interest rates, making car loans and student loans more expensive. This economic squeeze is one of the biggest challenges facing President Trump's administration right now.

world-news.title: US court pauses decision blocking Trump's 10 percent global tariff
world-news.summary: President Trump wants to place a 10 percent tariff—a tax—on all goods imported into the United States from other countries. A coalition of 24 US states challenged this policy in court, arguing it violates the Trade Act of 1974, a law that limits the president's power over trade. The court has temporarily paused its decision, meaning the tariff remains in effect for now while the legal battle continues. Tariffs make foreign products more expensive, which can protect American companies but also raise prices for consumers buying everything from clothes to electronics. This matters to teenagers because higher tariffs mean higher prices at stores. The dispute also affects international relations—other countries like China and Europe are threatening to place their own tariffs on American products in response, potentially starting a trade war that hurts the global economy.

world-news.title: Lebanon says two paramedics among 13 killed in Israeli strikes
world-news.summary: Israeli military strikes in Lebanon killed 13 people, including two paramedics who were trying to rescue survivors from an earlier attack in the city of Nabatieh. This incident illustrates how the Israel-Iran conflict is spreading beyond Israel into neighboring countries like Lebanon, which sits on Israel's northern border. Lebanon is home to Hezbollah, a militant group supported by Iran, making it a flashpoint in the larger regional conflict. The killing of emergency responders—people trying to save lives—shows how intense this conflict has become and how difficult it is for civilians to stay safe. This matters globally because the Middle East is one of the world's most important regions for oil and international trade; instability here affects everyone. The conflict also raises humanitarian concerns about civilian casualties and whether international law protecting aid workers is being respected.

world-news.title: Zelensky's ex-chief of staff in court as Ukraine corruption probe escalates
world-news.summary: Andriy Yermak, the former chief of staff (top advisor) to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, has been named as a suspect in a money-laundering scheme by Ukraine's anti-corruption agencies. Money laundering means illegally hiding the source of money, often from crime or corruption, to make it look legitimate. This is a significant moment because Ukraine is fighting a war against Russia while also trying to clean up its government and prove it's not corrupt—something Western countries like the US care about when deciding whether to send military aid. The investigation shows that even as Ukraine battles an invasion, internal corruption remains a serious problem. This matters because it affects international support: countries giving billions of dollars to help Ukraine fight Russia want to know their money isn't being stolen. Corruption investigations can also distract from the war effort and create political instability when a country needs unity most.

world-news.title: Trump downplays US-Iran differences as he heads to Beijing to meet with Xi
world-news.summary: As President Trump travels to China, his administration is sending mixed messages about how serious the Iran war really is. Some officials say the Iran conflict is a top priority, while Trump himself is playing it down, suggesting that trade issues with China are more important. This confusion reveals tensions within Trump's administration about whether military conflicts or economic competition should be the main focus. The Iran war has killed thousands in Lebanon and is causing global inflation, yet Trump seems to be prioritizing US-China trade negotiations over the Middle East crisis. This matters because other countries watch America's priorities to understand what alliances matter and what threats are real. Mixed signals from the US president can confuse allies and enemies alike, potentially making international conflicts harder to resolve. It also shows how the US is juggling multiple global crises simultaneously—war, inflation, and superpower competition.

world-news.title: Uganda's president sworn in for record seventh term
world-news.summary: Yoweri Museveni, Uganda's 81-year-old president, has been sworn in for his seventh term after winning disputed elections in January 2026. Disputed means the elections were controversial and many people questioned whether they were fair and free. Museveni has ruled Uganda for 40 years, making him one of Africa's longest-serving leaders—most democracies have term limits preventing this. Uganda's elections were criticized by international observers who said there were irregularities and concerns about fairness. This matters because it reflects a broader trend in Africa where some leaders hold power far longer than their constitutions allow, limiting democratic freedoms. When elections aren't fair, citizens lose faith in government, and international partners become concerned about stability and human rights. Museveni's seventh term raises questions about whether Uganda respects democratic principles and whether the international community will accept the election results or impose consequences.

world-news.title: South Africa's top court bars repeat asylum applications
world-news.summary: South Africa's highest court has ruled that people cannot repeatedly apply for asylum (protection for refugees fleeing persecution) if their previous applications were rejected. The country's Home Affairs Minister called this a victory against abusing the refugee system. Asylum seekers are people fleeing war, persecution, or violence in their home countries who ask other nations for safety and legal status. This ruling makes it harder for rejected applicants to try again, even if circumstances change or new evidence emerges. This matters globally because South Africa is a major destination for African refugees fleeing conflicts in countries like Zimbabwe, Syria, and Somalia. The decision could set a precedent affecting how other countries handle asylum claims. It raises humanitarian concerns: while protecting against fraud, the ruling might also prevent legitimate refugees from getting protection. Immigration and refugee policy is increasingly important as global conflicts and climate change force more people to seek safety across borders.

world-news.title: Peru presidential candidate Roberto Sanchez charged with financial crimes
world-news.summary: Roberto Sanchez, a leftist presidential candidate in Peru, has been charged with financial crimes including false financial disclosures—meaning he allegedly lied about his money on official documents. Prosecutors are calling for him to be jailed for five years and four months. This accusation is significant because Peru is preparing for elections, and Sanchez was a major candidate, meaning this charge affects the country's political future. Financial crimes undermine trust in government and elections; voters need to know their leaders are honest. Peru has experienced significant political instability in recent years with multiple presidents facing corruption charges, reflecting broader problems with governance in the country. This case matters internationally because it shows challenges in Latin American democracies where corruption is common and political leaders frequently face legal troubles. Such instability can affect trade relationships, investment, and regional cooperation in South America.

energy-climate.title: Fire hits German home fitted with photovoltaic tiles
energy-climate.summary: The fire spread beneath the roof structure and required extensive manual dismantling of PV-integrated components before being fully extinguished. A firefighter was injured by electric shock during the blaze.

energy-climate.title: Jolywood starts mass production of low-silver TOPCon solar cells
energy-climate.summary: The Chinese manufacturer said its novel TOPCon cell design enables partial replacement of silver with aluminum paste while maintaining TOPCon-level efficiency of over 27% and compatibility with existing production lines.

energy-climate.title: Zambia’s largest solar plant goes online
energy-climate.summary: The 136 MW Itimpi II solar plant is Zambia’s largest operational solar project to date. Elsewhere in the country, an investment agreement has been signed to add 250 MW of solar to the 100 MW Nambala solar power plant.

energy-climate.title: The impact of fly ash-induced soiling on PV panel performance
energy-climate.summary: Scientists in Morocco have conceived an experimental–numerical model to quantify how fly ash soiling affects photovoltaic (PV) modules, capturing both optical losses and thermal effects. Their findings show that while dust layers can reduce panel temperature, fly ash significantly degrades efficiency in a non-linear way, highlighting the need for predictive models for real-world solar performance.

energy-climate.title: Ford Energy announces DC block utility-scale BESS based on 512 Ah LFP cells

energy-climate.title: How hail is rewriting the solar insurance rulebook
energy-climate.summary: Insurers tell pv magazine that severe convective storms (SCS) drove $60 billion in insured losses in 2025, a mounting toll that has become the primary force tightening capacity and raising prices across the solar energy insurance market.

energy-climate.title: All-perovskite tandem solar cell built with laser polishing achieves 29.80% efficiency
energy-climate.summary: Chinese researchers developed an all-perovskite tandem solar cell using a non-contact laser polishing strategy that reduces surface defects and improves charge extraction in lead-tin perovskite films. The device achieved up to 29.80% tandem efficiency, with the bottom cell improving from 19.64% to 24.07% and showing strong operational stability under continuous illumination.

Australia’s mining giants are aiding China’s push to internationalise its currency and reduce the dominance of the US dollar, as they gradually shift towards using the yuan for financing and settlements, analysts said.
China has been using its heft as the world’s dominant iron ore buyer to push global mining companies to adopt the yuan. And several firms are already making the switch, with China’s relatively low interest rates becoming an added incentive.

asia-pacific.title: China actor who has stopped growing at 9 is known for playing child roles to build acting career
asia-pacific.summary:
An actor in China nicknamed the “ageless man” because of a childhood condition which halted his growth has silenced detractors with his nuanced performances.
According to Beiqing.com, Hou Xiang, 40, from Beijing, was born prematurely after his mother suffered from malnutrition during pregnancy.
His growth and voice development stopped around the age of nine, leaving him less than 1.6 metres tall.
Even in adulthood, strangers often mistake Hou for a boy, asking his age or where he studies.
